1:
The witnesses open the chapter on two fronts at once — the tangle of its chronology and the moral state of the kingdom it introduces. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke does the arithmetic that later verses will complicate: Jehoahaz began to reign at the very commencement of the twenty-third year of Joash of Judah and reigned seventeen years, fourteen of them alone and three in association with his son Joash — a reckoning laid down in advance to explain the apparent discrepancy at v.10. The reformed John Gill fixes the same date by observing that it fell in the very year Joash of Judah was so zealous and busy in repairing the temple, so that the two Joashes, of Judah and of Israel, come to overlap.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the bare regnal notice into a long meditation on Israel's buried glory. It had been Israel's honour to worship the only living and true God, and to be taken under the special protection of heaven; but by clinging to the calves of Jeroboam they changed the glory of their incorruptible God into the similitude of an ox, and the truth of God into a lie, levelling themselves with the nations that worship the work of their own hands. "How is the gold become dim!" he laments; the debauching of a nation is certainly the debasing of it, and a people once defended by God is now exposed to the insults of all its neighbours. In all the history of the ten tribes, he notes, we never find the least shock given to that idolatry — in every reign the calf was still their god.

Yet Henry refuses to read the reign as unrelieved shame. Some sparks of Israel's ancient honour still glow in the ashes: it was the honour of Israel that they were a praying people, and that they had God near to them in all that they called upon him for, a nearness he grounds in the ancient boast of Deut. 4:7. Even here, he insists, God is swift to show mercy, willing to find a reason to be gracious, and ready to look as far back as the covenant with Abraham which Israel had so often broken and forfeited — the covenant God pledged to remember in Lev. 26:42. The voices do not divide; together they set the frame for the whole chapter, in which a sunk and idolatrous kingdom is nonetheless not abandoned, because the God against whom it sins is the God who keeps covenant.
2:
The verse is one of the recurring verdicts of the book — "he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD, and followed the sins of Jeroboam" — and the witnesses treat it both as a settled formula and as the key to everything that follows. The reformed John Gill glosses the evil in a single word: he committed idolatry, keeping up the calf-worship that his fathers had made the state religion of the northern kingdom. The formula is not new, but its weight lies in the closing clause, "he departed not therefrom" — the sin is not a lapse but a fixed and inherited policy.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch first settle the chronology, reasoning that if Jehu died early in the twenty-eighth year of his reign, his son's accession may fall in the closing months of the twenty-first year of Joash of Judah, so that Jehoahaz reigned some sixteen years and a few months, described in round terms as seventeen. But their deeper interest is theological. Because Jehoahaz trod in the footsteps of his forefathers and continued the sin of Jeroboam, the Lord punished Israel during his reign even more than in that of his predecessor: the longer and the more obstinately the sin was persisted in, the more severe the punishment became. Here is the law the chapter will illustrate — the calf that seemed a harmless matter of state was, in the sight of God, the engine of the nation's ruin.

That punishment takes concrete form at once: God gave them into the power of the Syrian king Hazael and his son Benhadad, Keil and Delitzsch note, for "the whole time" of the reign of Jehoahaz — a phrase they carry forward to the summary of v.22. The two witnesses do not contend. They agree that the evaluation is not the historian's private disapproval but God's own judgment, and that the persistence of the sin — "he departed not" — is precisely what draws down the escalating oppression under which the whole reign will groan. The tragedy is that the very idolatry meant to secure the kingdom's independence from Jerusalem is what delivers it, bound and disarmed, into the hand of Damascus.
3:
"And the anger of the LORD was kindled against Israel" — the witnesses read the sentence as the hinge between the nation's guilt and its affliction, and they refuse to let the people hide behind their kings. The reformed John Gill puts the point tersely: God's anger burned against Israel because they were "doing as their kings did." The idolatry was not merely a royal policy imposed from above; the people walked in it willingly, so that the whole nation, and not the throne alone, stands under the kindled wrath.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace the shape of the judgment and its surprising turn. The Lord delivered Israel into the hand of Hazael king of Syria, and into the hand of Benhadad the son of Hazael, all their days; the two names mark a sustained oppression running across a generation. Yet the very next movement is mercy: in this oppression Jehoahaz prayed to the Lord, and the Lord heard him, because he saw their oppression at the hands of the Syrians, and gave Israel a saviour, so that they came out from the power of Syria and dwelt in their booths again as before — able, that is, to live peaceably in their homes without being driven off and led away by the foe.

Keil and Delitzsch are careful about the identity of that saviour, rejecting the older guesses — an angel, the prophet Elisha, one of Joash's captains, or a single victory won by Jehoahaz himself — and locating him rather in the two successors of Jehoahaz, the kings Jehoash and Jeroboam, through whom the deliverance would come by stages. They note too the shape of the relief: Israel "dwelt in their booths again, as before," able once more to live peaceably at home without being driven off and led away by the foe. The oppression by Hazael and his son Benhadad is described as lasting "all their days," a phrase they carry across the whole reign, so that the mercy breaks into a darkness that was otherwise unbroken.

The two witnesses land together on the chapter's governing rhythm: sin kindles wrath, wrath sends the Syrian rod, and even under the rod a distressed king may cry out and be heard. Gill is careful that the mercy takes nothing from the justice — the anger was righteous, the people were guilty with their kings, and the deliverance when it comes will be pure grace and not reward. The anger of God is real and just, but it is not his last word; already in this verse the promise of a saviour is planted in the soil of judgment, to be drawn out in the verses that follow, where the prayer of v.4 and the saviour of v.5 unfold what is here compressed into a single sentence.
4:
The verse is the chapter's first great turn from wrath to mercy — "Jehoahaz besought the LORD, and the LORD hearkened unto him" — and the witnesses gather around both the king's prayer and God's motive for hearing it. The reformed John Gill stresses where the distressed king did not go: he did not apply to the calves he worshipped, but to the Lord; and the Lord regarded his prayer not for his sake, nor for any righteousness or even any true repentance in him, for his humiliation was only external, but because Israel were oppressed, and they were still his people and he their God, however sadly they had departed from him.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the same contrast into a lesson on prayer. It becomes kings, he says, to be beggars at God's door, and the greatest of men to be humble petitioners at the footstool of his throne; need will drive them to it. He marvels at the swiftness of the mercy — how ready God is to hear, how willing to find out a reason to be gracious, so willing that he looks all the way back to the ancient covenant Israel had so often broken and forfeited. Let this, Henry urges, invite and engage us for ever to him, and encourage even those who have forsaken him to return, "for there is forgiveness with him, that he may be feared."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds the philology and a caution about the timing. "Besought" is literally "stroked the face of," a vivid metaphor of entreaty that recurs in Exod. 32:11 and 1 Kings 13:6; and "for he saw the oppression" echoes the language of the Exodus deliverance in Exod. 3:7 and Deut. 26:7. But the answer, Ellicott insists, was not immediate: the Syrian invasions were renewed again and again throughout the reign of Jehoahaz, and the tide only began to turn under Joash and became permanent under Jeroboam II, so that the deliverance of v.5 was a slow-ripening fruit of this prayer. The "king of Syria" is left intentionally general, to embrace both Hazael and his son Benhadad. The voices agree that the prayer was heard, and heard on the ground not of the man's worth but of God's compassion for an afflicted people whom he had bound himself, by covenant, to spare.
5:
"And the LORD gave Israel a saviour" — and here the witnesses openly divide, not over the fact of deliverance but over the man through whom it came. The reformed John Gill and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke both identify the saviour as Joash, the son of Jehoahaz, whose successful wars against the Syrians are recorded at the close of the chapter. Gill rules out the alternatives one by one — not an angel sent from heaven, nor a captain raised up among them, nor the prophet Elisha who merely predicted the deliverance, nor Jehoahaz himself, but his son Joash. Clarke agrees flatly: "this was undoubtedly Joash," and even commends Houbigant's proposal to reorder the verses for a smoother sense.

The other grammatical-historical witness, Charles Ellicott, looks a generation further and names Jeroboam II, the grandson of Jehoahaz, a vigorous and successful sovereign of whom it is expressly said that Jehovah "saved" Israel by his hand in 2 Kings 14:27. Keil and Delitzsch decline to choose between father and son, taking the saviour to be given in both the successors of Jehoahaz, the kings Jehoash and Jeroboam together, through whom the recovery unfolded by stages. So the debate is real: was the promised deliverer Joash, whose three victories fulfil Elisha's word, or Jeroboam II, under whom the borders were finally restored, or both as a single dynastic gift?

On the meaning of the deliverance the witnesses are one. Ellicott explains the sequel — "the children of Israel dwelt in their tents, as beforetime" — as a return to the open country, for in time of war they had been driven to take refuge in strongholds and fortified cities; now they could dwell again at peace in the countryside. Keil and Delitzsch add that this is stated as a parenthesis, framed by the reminder that even so "they departed not from the sins of the house of Jeroboam." The mercy, in other words, was pure grace: it did not wait upon reformation, and it did not produce it. God gave a saviour to a people who went on worshipping the calves, and the recovery he grants here is the seed of the full recovery recorded at v.25.
6:
"Nevertheless they departed not from the sins of the house of Jeroboam" — the witnesses read this verse as the sharp underside of the mercy just granted, the ingratitude of a people delivered who will not part with their idols. The reformed John Gill calls it plainly an instance of great ingratitude: they continued to worship the calves still, even after God had heard their king and given them a saviour. He notes the textual variant carried by the Syriac and Arabic versions, which read "he departed not," making Jehoahaz the subject rather than the nation — though the sense is much the same, whether it is the king or the people who cling to the sin.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the point that the restoration of divine favour did not issue in the abolition of the irregular worship Jeroboam I had established as the state religion of the north; and he adds the historian's own vantage, writing long afterward from the point of view of the exile. He gives close attention to the last clause, "there remained the grove also in Samaria," rendering it rather "moreover the Asherah stood in Samaria" — the sacred tree so often depicted in Assyrian art, symbol of the productive principle of nature and sacred to Ashtoreth. With the return of peace and the renewal of prosperity, he observes, luxury reappeared, and the idolatry that specially countenanced it lifted up its head again, a pattern he ties to the fuller indictment of 2 Kings 17:16.

The wesleyan-arminian voices fill in the history of that grove. Adam Clarke identifies it as Asherah, or Astarte, still standing and worshipped in Samaria with all her abominable rites; John Wesley traces it back to the grove Ahab had planted for the worship of Baal, which should long since have been destroyed under the command of Deut. 7:5. Keil and Delitzsch agree that the Asherah had stood at Samaria probably from the days of Ahab in 1 Kings 16:33, since Jehu, for all his zeal, is not said to have destroyed it. The voices do not divide; together they render a portrait of a nation that will take God's deliverance and keep its idols too, so that peace and prosperity only feed the corruption they were meant to heal.
7:
The verse measures the depth of Israel's humiliation in a single grim inventory — "fifty horsemen, and ten chariots, and ten thousand footmen" — and the witnesses read it both as military history and as the arithmetic of judgment. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had already seized on it: the militia of the whole kingdom, all the force they could bring into the field, was reduced to this despicable muster, so that he cries, "Have the thousands of Israel come to this?" The Syrians had plucked and pillaged them until the goodness of their land only tempted their neighbours to prey upon them — the debasing, as he elsewhere says, of a debauched nation.

The grammatical-historical witnesses fix the grammar and the strategy. Keil and Delitzsch read the verse as furnishing the ground for the deliverance of v.5: God gave them a saviour, although they did not desist from the sin of Jeroboam, for Israel had been brought to the last extremity — the Lord had left to Jehoahaz only fifty horsemen and the rest, and the king of Syria "had made them like dust for trampling upon," an expression, they say, denoting utter destruction. Charles Ellicott takes the subject of "he had not left" to be Jehovah himself, and reads the tiny remnant as the result of some great battle or of successive engagements: the destruction of precisely these arms — chariots and cavalry, in which the Syrians were especially strong — amounted to a complete disarmament that rendered further resistance hopeless. His rendering, "set them like the dust to trample on," recalls the trodden-down enemy of 2 Samuel 22:43 and Isaiah 10:6.

A small difference of reading runs beneath the agreement. The reformed John Gill makes the king of Syria the one who left Israel so few, connecting the verse as a parenthesis to the surrounding narrative; the grammatical-historical witnesses prefer Jehovah as the subject, so that the disarmament is itself a stroke of God's hand and not merely of Damascus. But the schools converge on the point that matters: whether the immediate agent is named as Hazael or as the LORD who used him, the reduction of a whole nation's army to fifty horsemen is the visible shape of the anger kindled in v.3, and the measure of how much the coming deliverance would owe to sheer grace and not to any strength of Israel's own.
8:
The verse is one of the book's standing closing-formulas — "the rest of the acts of Jehoahaz, and all that he did, and his might" — yet the witnesses find in that single word might a genuine theological difficulty and its resolution. The grammatical-historical voices take it first as plain history. Charles Ellicott glosses "his might" as prowess, referring it to the king's wars with the Syrians. Keil and Delitzsch agree that Jehoahaz had probably shown his might in the war with Syria, although he had been overcome — and it is that concession, valour joined to defeat, that the other witnesses seize upon.

For the difficulty is obvious: if the king was mighty and valiant, why was he beaten? The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley answers it directly. Though his success was not good, Jehoahaz showed much personal valour; and this, Wesley says, is noted on purpose "to intimate that the Israelites were not conquered because of the cowardice of their king, but merely from the righteous judgment of God, who was now resolved to reckon with them for their apostasy." The defeat, in other words, was judicial, not martial. The reformed John Gill lands on exactly the same note from the other side: Jehoahaz was a man of courage who exerted himself against the Syrians, yet was not successful "because the Lord was not with him, but against him."

So the witnesses, few as they are here, weave a single instructive cord. The formula that seems merely to defer to the royal annals — the details, Keil and Delitzsch observe, are left to the common chronicles while Scripture presses on to what concerns the prophet — in fact carries a pointed lesson. A brave king may fight hard and still lose, when God has set himself against a people for their sin. The mightiest arm is nothing when the Lord withdraws, and Israel's ruin is traced not to any failure of nerve on the throne but to the withdrawn favour of heaven. Thus even this bare notice serves the chapter's argument, that the nation's affliction and its coming relief alike lie wholly in the hand of God.
9:
"And Jehoahaz slept with his fathers; and they buried him in Samaria: and Joash his son reigned in his stead." The witnesses have little to add to so conventional a death-notice, and they say so honestly, but the little they do say opens the formula's quiet dignity. The reformed John Gill notes only that the king "died, as they did" — the phrase "slept with his fathers" being the settled biblical idiom for going the way of one's ancestors, the common lot of kings good and bad alike.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens that observation into a caution against reading too much peace into the words. "Slept with his fathers" means simply "lay down to sleep, as his fathers had done before him"; and, he points out, the very same phrase is used even of Amaziah, who came to a violent end at 2 Kings 14:22. The idiom, then, marks burial among one's people, not necessarily a quiet or honoured death; it is a formula of continuity, not a verdict on the man. Keil and Delitzsch simply carry the narrative forward, joining this burial in Samaria to the accession of Joash and the opening of the next reign.

Yet the sparse notice is not without pathos when set in the chapter's frame. Jehoahaz is buried in Samaria, the capital of the calf-kingdom whose idolatry he had faithfully continued; the dynasty of Jehu goes quietly on, one evil king laid in the grave and his son set on the throne to walk the same road. The formula gives him the outward dignity of rest with his fathers, but the reign it closes was one of oppression, disarmament, and unremitting Syrian pressure. There is a further irony that the chapter itself will draw out: this king "slept with his fathers" in the ordinary way of all flesh, while the prophet whose death is soon to be told would, even from the grave, become the instrument of a resurrection. The one who held the throne passes into silence; the one who held the word of God does not. So the brief obituary, drawing on the verse's own words and its place in the story, prepares the reader to weigh a king's death against a prophet's, and to ask where the true power in Israel had all along resided.
10:
The accession of Joash of Israel opens on a chronological knot, and the witnesses take opposite roads to untie it. The verse dates his reign to "the thirty-seventh year of Joash king of Judah," but his father Jehoahaz had begun in the twenty-third and reigned seventeen years, which should place the son in the thirty-ninth or fortieth. The reformed John Gill and the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke both solve it by a co-regency: Gill supposes the son reigned two or three years in his father's lifetime, being raised up to be a saviour of Israel from the Syrians, so that Jehoahaz lived to see his own prayer answered; Clarke, citing Calmet, says Joash was associated with his father two years before his death and succeeded him fully some two years later, the sixteen years of his reign including the co-regency.

The other grammatical-historical witness prefers a textual answer. Charles Ellicott notes bluntly that "the thirty-seventh year" does not agree with the figures of v.1, and that the Aldine text of the Septuagint reads "thirty-ninth," which he judges to be right; he adds that the Hebrew is briefer than the English, saying simply "reigned sixteen years." So the debate is drawn between reconciliation by co-regency, which harmonizes the received numbers, and emendation, which corrects one figure to fit the rest.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry passes over the arithmetic to weigh the man. Joash was none of the worst of Israel's kings, yet because he kept up the ancient and politic idolatry of the house of Jeroboam it is written that "he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD" — for that one sin, however little evil men saw in it, was in the sight of the Lord a very wicked thing, and "his judgment is according to truth." Henry marvels at the economy of the inspired penman, who passes lightly over the king's acts and his might in war, and dwells instead on the respect he showed to Elisha: "one good action shall make a better figure in God's book than twenty great ones," so that it gains a man a better reputation to honour a prophet than to conquer a king and his army. The witnesses thus give the verse two frames at once — a chronological puzzle solved by co-regency or by textual correction, and a moral portrait in which a middling king's whole standing before God turns on his idolatry and his one act of reverence toward the dying man of God.
11:
"And he did that which was evil in the sight of the LORD; he departed not from all the sins of Jeroboam." The witnesses treat this repeated verdict as the dynasty's inescapable refrain, and they read it with a single mind. The reformed John Gill notes only that Joash is described "in the same words" as his father — the standing formula recurring unchanged, because the sin recurs unchanged. There is a terrible monotony in it: reign after reign, the same evaluation, the same calf-worship, the same failure to depart.

The grammatical-historical witnesses attend to the sequel and to a nicety of grammar. Keil and Delitzsch link the verse forward to the fuller record it presupposes, observing that the rest of Joash's acts and his might in the war against Amaziah king of Judah are related in the history of that king at 2 Kings 14:8 and following, and that the close of his reign is likewise given there with the standing formula. Charles Ellicott notes the Hebrew idiom of the last clause, "in it he walked," where the pronoun is collective in force — it is the conduct of the nation, not merely the king, that is being described, so that the whole people walk on in the sin their throne perpetuates.

That grammatical point carries the theological weight the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had already pressed: it was that one evil — the politic idolatry of the calves — that fastened an indelible mark of infamy upon the king's name, however little evil men saw in it, because God's judgment is according to truth and not according to men's estimate. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the verse is no mere formula but a real charge, that its force falls on king and people together, and that the persistence of the sin "he departed not" is precisely what keeps the northern kingdom under the shadow that has hung over it since the anger of God was kindled earlier in the chapter. Joash, like his father, will yet be granted mercy and victory; but the historian will not let the reader forget on what unreformed soil that mercy falls.
12:
Another of the book's closing-formulas — "the rest of the acts of Joash, and all that he did, and his might wherewith he fought against Amaziah king of Judah" — draws the witnesses to the war between the two Hebrew kingdoms that the verse names but does not narrate. The reformed John Gill remarks on the irony that as yet none of Joash's acts have been recorded except his sinful ones; the annals promised here still lie ahead, and the reader is pointed elsewhere for the deeds themselves.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke supplies from the parallel history the war the verse only mentions. The conflict with Amaziah, told in full at 2 Chronicles 25, ended in the total defeat of Amaziah, who was taken prisoner by Joash and afterwards slain in a conspiracy at Lachish; Joash took Jerusalem, broke down four hundred cubits of its wall, and carried off the royal treasures and the treasures of the house of God, as recounted in 2 Chron. 25:20-27. It is a grim episode of brother against brother, Israel humbling Judah and plundering the Temple, which the Israelite annalist here compresses into the single word "might."

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott attends to the wording and its cross-references. The whole notice is repeated at 2 Kings 14:15-16, where the reign of Joash is properly closed; and the phrase "wherewith he fought" is better rendered "how he fought," with a conjunction that should be restored here from the parallel. The war "against Amaziah" is the one narrated more fully at 2 Kings 14:8 and following, where the challenge of the Judean king and his humbling defeat are told at length. That this same closing-formula appears twice — here in anticipation and again in its proper place — reflects the historian's method throughout the chapter, gathering the record of a reign into a compact notice before turning aside to the matter he most wishes to relate.

The witnesses do not contend; together they let the formula do its proper work, deferring the martial details to the fuller record while the sacred writer keeps his own narrative fixed on what matters most to him — not the king's victories over Judah, but his last meeting with the prophet Elisha, to which the next verses turn. The reticence is itself a judgment of proportion: the conqueror of Jerusalem earns a single line, while the honourer of the dying man of God is granted a whole scene. A king may break down the walls of the holy city and carry off the treasures of the house of God, and Scripture will pass it by in a phrase; but let him weep at a prophet's bedside, and the record lingers. In that unequal weighing the chapter quietly teaches whose deeds truly endure.
13:
"And Joash slept with his fathers; and Jeroboam sat upon his throne: and Joash was buried in Samaria with the kings of Israel." With only two witnesses at hand, the reading is modest, but they catch what is unusual in a formula the book usually keeps rigid. The reformed John Gill glosses the opening clause simply — Joash "died" — the familiar idiom of the royal obituary.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fastens on the wording that breaks the pattern. The stereotyped phrase would be "and Jeroboam his son reigned in his stead"; instead the text says "Jeroboam sat upon his throne," a variation Ellicott calls remarkable and compares with the regular form at 2 Kings 14:16. He records the conjecture of the Talmud and of Kimchi, who fancy that the unusual phrase implies Joash associated Jeroboam with himself on the throne for fear of a revolt — an inference Ellicott plainly finds strained. And he notes that Joash was "buried in Samaria with the kings of Israel," so that there were "tombs of the kings" in the northern capital, as there were at Jerusalem — the calf-kingdom aping even in death the dignities of David's city.

The placement of this death-notice is itself worth remark, and Keil and Delitzsch (in their comment on the surrounding verses) had explained it: the close of Joash's reign properly belongs at the end of the chapter, and is indeed repeated at 2 Kings 14:15-16, but the historian introduces it early here because the verses that follow are bound up with the death of Elisha, and he wishes to wind up the formal record of the reign before turning to the prophet. So the obituary of Joash is set down out of its strict chronological place, as a kind of parenthesis, precisely so that the great scene of Elisha's last sickness may stand undisturbed. The witnesses do not divide; they leave us with a king laid among his royal fathers, a dynasty passing smoothly to Jeroboam, and the historian's deliberate clearing of the stage for the one death in this chapter that will matter more than any king's — the death of the man of God whose bones still held the power of life.
14:
The chapter turns from thrones to a sickbed, and the witnesses gather with unusual warmth around the dying Elisha and the weeping king. The reformed John Gill marks the plain fact that carries a quiet lesson: "the prophets do not live for ever"; this sickness was unto death, and Elisha died and was not translated as Elijah had been. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the Hebrew — "he was to die" — and pictures the scene: Joash "came down" to the prophet's house, a phrase like those at 2 Kings 5:24, and "wept over his face" as the old man lay upon his bed, lamenting the loss of one who by his teaching, his prayers, his counsel and his wonder-working power had been more to Israel than chariots and horsemen.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry and the wesleyan-arminian witnesses dwell on the length and the humility of the prophet's course. Elisha had lived long — some sixty years, Henry and Wesley reckon, since he was first called — a burning and a shining light, where Elijah had finished his testimony in a fourth part of that time; and yet from the anointing of Jehu, forty-five years back, we hear nothing of him until this deathbed. Henry draws the comfort out: "Let not old people complain of obscurity, but rather be well pleased with retirement"; a man may be useful to the last though no longer famous. And though the spirit of Elijah had rested on Elisha, he was not caught up in a fiery chariot but went the common road out of the world, visited with the visitation of all men — for if God honour some above others who are not their inferiors in grace, who shall find fault? Adam Clarke adds only that this fell about the tenth year of Joash, so that Elisha had prophesied some sixty-five years.

On the king's tears the witnesses hold two truths together. That Joash came and wept was, Henry says, real evidence of good in him: he loved and honoured a faithful prophet, so far from persecuting him as a troubler of Israel; and it is instructive to attend the deathbeds of good men, that we may learn to die. Henry notes too that the king borrowed his very lament — "My father, my father, the chariot of Israel, and the horsemen thereof" — from the words Elisha himself had cried when Elijah was taken up in 2 Kings 2:12, and that those who honour the generation before them are often so honoured by the generation after, watered as they watered, in the promise of Prov. 11:25. Yet Henry will not gild the motive: the king lamented Elisha as "the chariot and horsemen of Israel" because the prophet was the kingdom's true defence, and he could ill be spared when Israel had but fifty horsemen and ten chariots left, as v.7 has just told. Reverence and self-interest mingle in the royal grief; and the witnesses agree that Ellicott's verdict stands over it all — this dying man had been worth more to Israel than all her cavalry.
15:
From his deathbed Elisha calls for bow and arrows, and the witnesses read the demand as the opening of a prophetic sign-act, a piece of acted prophecy whose meaning is given entirely by God. The reformed John Gill notes the homely realism: these were the usual instruments of war in that day, and if none were kept in the prophet's house the king could have them from the guards who attended him. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott agrees that the bow and arrows came "from one of the royal attendants" — the scene is not staged with special props but seizes on the ordinary weapons at hand.

Keil and Delitzsch lay out the whole action of which this is the first step. "Take bow and arrows," said the prophet, "and let thy hand pass over the bow" — that is, stretch it; then he directed the king to open the eastward window and shoot, and named the arrow as it flew "an arrow of salvation from the LORD, and an arrow of salvation against the Syrians," with the promise that Joash would smite Syria at Aphek — the old battlefield of 1 Kings 20:26 — to their destruction. The arrow, they explain, was to be a symbol of the LORD's help against Syria; and this promise the king was then to appropriate to himself by a further act of his own, the striking of the arrows on the ground.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke draws the interpretive principle plainly: "the bow, the arrows, and the smiting on the ground were all emblematical things, indicative of the deliverance of Israel from Syria." Nothing here is idle theatre; each gesture is a divinely appointed sign, and the weapon in the king's hand becomes a pledge in miniature of a whole campaign. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that Elisha, dying, is not merely comforting a grieving king with brave words but enacting before him a real prophecy — that the God who is about to bury his servant will not bury his cause, but will use even the trembling of a deathbed to launch Israel's deliverance. The sign begins in the taking up of a common bow; its weight will fall on how the king handles what the prophet gives.
16:
"Put thine hand upon the bow... and Elisha put his hands upon the king's hands." The witnesses concentrate on that laying-on of the dying prophet's hands, and read in it the whole theology of the sign. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott begins with the mechanics of archery: in drawing a bow the left hand "rides" upon it while the right grasps arrow and string, so the king takes the proper posture of a shooter. But the crux is what Elisha does next. He laid his own hands upon the king's hands, Ellicott explains, "so as to invest the act of shooting with a prophetic character," and further to signify the consecration of the king to the task the shooting symbolised — though, he cautiously adds, it is not implied that the prophet's hands were still upon the king's when the arrow actually flew.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws out the spiritual sense of the gesture. Elisha put his hands upon the king's hands to signify that in all his expeditions against the Syrians the king must look up to God for direction and strength, reckoning his own hands not sufficient for him but going on in dependence upon divine aid — for it is God who "teacheth my hands to war," as the psalmist confesses in Ps. 18:34 and Ps. 144:1. And here Henry finds the moving paradox of the scene: "the trembling hands of a dying prophet, as they signified the concurrence and communication of the power of God, gave this arrow more force than the hands of the king in his full strength." The feeble hands that could scarcely hold the bow carried more of heaven's power than the strong young hands beneath them.

The reformed John Gill adds only the small observing detail that it was the king's left hand that went upon the bow, the hand that steadies while the right draws. The witnesses do not divide; they agree that the covering of the king's hands by the prophet's is the visible pledge that the victory to come is the LORD's and not the archer's. The strength for the deliverance of Israel flows not from the throne but from the God whose servant lies dying, mediated through hands too weak to fight but strong enough, under God, to bless. It is a lesson the king will need, for the next verses will test whether he has grasped that all his conquests must be received, like this arrow, from a hand above his own.
17:
The arrow flies eastward at Elisha's word — "the arrow of the LORD's deliverance, and the arrow of deliverance from Syria" — and the witnesses read the shot as at once a declaration of war and a promise of victory. The reformed John Gill and wesleyan-arminian John Wesley fix the geography: the window was opened toward Syria, which lay north-east of Israel, and toward the eastward country the Syrians had seized, which this sign showed would be recovered. Wesley reads the flying arrow simply as "a token what God intended to do against them."

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke enriches the scene with the antiquity behind the gesture. The window opened toward the country beyond Jordan that Hazael had taken; and the shooting of the arrow, he shows, drew on an ancient custom of hurling a dart or spear into a land one meant to invade. He cites Justin, that Alexander the Great, arriving on the Ionian coast, "threw a dart as into a hostile country"; Virgil, who has Turnus open battle by flinging his spear; and Servius, who records how the Roman pater patratus would declare war by casting a spear across the enemy's border. So the arrow of Joash is a solemn, God-directed act of war: "as surely as that arrow is shot toward the lands conquered from Israel, so surely shall those lands be reconquered and restored."

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the two meanings the shot carries. It was at once a commission to the king to attack the Syrians notwithstanding their power and possession, and a promise of success in the attempt — "it is God that commands deliverance; and when he will effect it, who can hinder?" — for the LORD shoots out his arrows and the work is done, as in Ps. 18:14, and the territory in view is the eastward land lost in 2 Kings 10:33. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott renders the phrase "an arrow of victory for Jehovah, and an arrow of victory over Aram," and locates the promised triumph at Aphek — the scene of former defeats, named in Joshua 13:4 and 1 Kings 20:26 — now to become the place of Israel's victory; the words "till thou have consumed them," he adds, foretell the annihilation of the opposing army at Aphek, not of the entire forces of Syria, a limit that the next verses will make painfully important. The witnesses converge: the arrow is both summons and pledge, God's own declaration of war on Israel's oppressor and his guarantee of the outcome, if only the king will press the advantage he is given.
18:
The second sign follows the first — "Smite upon the ground. And he smote thrice, and stayed" — and the witnesses read the king's three strokes as the moment the promise is quietly measured out and, tragically, cut short. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott clarifies the action and its solemnity. The "arrows" are the bundle in the king's hand; "smite upon the ground" he renders "smite earthwards," as if striking a fallen enemy to the earth; and he notes that the king "smote thrice" — three being a sacred number, so that even the count is charged with significance. The Septuagint's fuller reading, "and Elisha said unto him," he judges more appropriate here, marking the start of a distinct second symbolic act.

The wesleyan-arminian witnesses draw out what the strokes were meant to signify. John Wesley states it crisply: the former sign, the arrow shot eastward, portended the victory; "this was to declare the number of the victories." Adam Clarke agrees that the smiting on the ground meant shooting or striking the arrows into it, and reflects on the king's reserve. The prophet knew this striking was emblematical; the king perhaps did not, and was unaware how much hung on the frequency of the act. Indeed, Clarke suggests, "perhaps it was of the Lord that he smote only thrice, as he had determined to give Israel those three victories only over the Syrians" — so that the king's restraint may itself have fallen within the secret purpose of God.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry supplies the psychology the bare verse withholds. The king smote thrice and no more, either out of a foolish tenderness toward the Syrians, striking as though afraid to hurt them, or because he thought it a silly, childish thing for a king to beat the floor with his arrows and reckoned thrice enough to humour a dying man. Either way, Henry says, "by contemning the sign he lost the thing signified." The witnesses do not finally divide over the fact — the king stopped at three — though they weigh its cause differently, between Clarke's providential limiting and Henry's diagnosis of half-heartedness. What they share is the sense of a promise offered without measure and received with a niggard hand, a gift of victory reduced by the very man it was meant to crown. The full grief of that reduction breaks out in the prophet's anger in the verse that follows.
19:
"And the man of God was wroth with him" — the dying prophet's anger over three strokes where five or six were wanted becomes for the witnesses a study in the meeting of human freedom and divine decree. The reformed John Gill explains why Elisha was angry: it had been revealed to the prophet that by the number of times the king smote the ground would be known how often he should prevail over his enemies, yet the matter was deliberately left to the king's own will — so that his smiting thrice and staying revealed his spirit, the coldness with which he would pursue the very victories God was pressing on him.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gives the fullest reading, setting the scene against the ancient practice of divination by arrows, which he says was of unknown antiquity in the Semitic world; shooting an arrow and observing where it fell was one way of seeking to fathom the purposes of the Power that overrules events, and he compares the arrows of David and Jonathan in 1 Samuel 20:35. The prophet left the number of strokes to the spontaneous impulse of the king because he believed the result, whatever it proved, would betoken the purpose of Jehovah — for "the lot is cast into the lap, but the whole disposing thereof is of the LORD," as Proverbs 16:33 has it. Elisha's anger, then, was "the natural anger of the man and the patriot, disappointed at the result of a divination from which he had hoped greater things"; and the sick prophet's irritability, Ellicott adds candidly, may have had its part too.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke guards against pressing that anger too far: "Elisha's being wroth because there were only three shots does not prove that God was wroth, or that he had intended to give Israel those three victories only." The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry laments the loss with a pastor's eye: "not being straitened in the power and promise of God, why should the king be straitened in his own expectations and endeavours?" It is a real trouble to good men, Henry says, to see those they wish well stand in their own light and forsake their own mercies. So the witnesses circle a genuine tension without wholly dissolving it — whether the outcome was fixed by the king's free and lukewarm act, so that he forfeited what fuller zeal would have won, or whether God had already decreed but three victories and overruled the king's hand to that end. Gill leans to the free act that revealed the king's heart; Clarke leans to the sovereign limit; Ellicott holds both, the impulse of the man and the disposing of the LORD, in a single frame. What none of them doubts is the sober warning the scene leaves: that faith may draw as deeply on the promise of God as it dares, and that a half-hearted hand may lose what a bolder one would have grasped.
20:
"And Elisha died, and they buried him." The witnesses pause over the plain fact of the prophet's death and the raiders that appear at the year's turn, few in number but attentive to what the words carry. The reformed John Gill gathers the traditions about the burial: it was in Samaria — Epiphanius names Sebaste — though some Jewish authorities placed it on Mount Carmel, in the cave of Elijah. On the dating he weighs the reckonings: by one Jewish chronology Elisha died in the tenth year of Joash, having prophesied more than sixty years, or sixty-six on Abarbanel's count from the nineteenth year of Jehoshaphat; yet Gill inclines with Kimchi to think he died early in Joash's reign, even before his father's death during the co-regency, and before any deliverance from the Syrians had been wrought.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch confirm the great span of the ministry: Elisha had been called by Elijah back in the reign of Ahab and did not die until the reign of Joash, so that more than forty years lay between his calling and his end. And they note the ominous detail with which the verse closes — "the bands of the Moabites invaded the land at the coming in of the year," the marauding parties that swept in annually, a standing token that Israel, for all the promise just given, still lay open to her enemies.

The witnesses do not divide, and the very restraint of the notice becomes eloquent. Elisha, on whom the double portion of Elijah's spirit had rested, does not follow his master into the whirlwind and the chariot of fire; he dies as other men die, is buried as other men are buried, and the world goes on with its raiding-parties as though nothing had changed. Yet the death of the prophet is set here, deliberately, beside the incursion of the plunderers, so that the reader feels the apparent triumph of death and the enemy at once. It is precisely against that backdrop — a buried prophet and a harried land — that the next verse will spring its astonishment. The grave that has just closed over Elisha is not, it turns out, the end of his power; and the God who let his servant die the common death of all men will show, over that servant's very bones, that he is still the LORD who kills and makes alive.
21:
The one great wonder of the chapter — a dead man revived at the touch of Elisha's bones — draws the witnesses to marvel, to explain, and, in one case, to polemicize. The grammatical-historical voices tell the story with vivid care. Charles Ellicott pictures a party of Israelites burying their dead when "the troop" of Moabite raiders suddenly appears; in their haste they cast the corpse into Elisha's sepulchre, a tomb the more easily opened, he suggests, comparing the readily rolled stone of Mark 16:3-4. Following Houbigant, he even reorders the Hebrew — "and they departed, and the man touched the bones" — and renders the outcome with the simplest of words, "and he lived." Keil and Delitzsch set the miracle in its narrative logic: it stands as the immediate seal of the promise Elisha had just uttered, a pledge that the LORD's word by his dying servant would surely come to pass.

The wesleyan-arminian voices press the meaning and the warning. John Wesley reads the miracle as wrought to honour the great prophet and to set a seal upon his doctrine — to strengthen the faith of Joash and of Israel in the promised success against Syria, and, in the midst of all their calamities, to comfort Elisha's followers with the hope of eternal life, of which this raising was a manifest pledge, and to awaken the careless to prepare for it. Adam Clarke turns the wonder into a lesson and a rebuke: it shows that the prophet did not work his miracles by any power of his own but by the power of God, who chose to honour his servant by making even his bones the instrument of a miracle after death. But Clarke will not let the point be abused. This, he insists, is the first and (he believes) the last true miracle ever performed by the bones of a dead man — "and yet on it, and such like, the whole system of miraculous working relics has been founded by the popish Church," a single, unrepeatable act pressed into the service of a whole false devotion.

The witnesses do not really divide; even Clarke's polemic rests on the point they all affirm, that the power was God's and not the relic's. Ellicott closes with the church father Bähr's fine summary: "Elisha died and was buried, like all other men, but even in death and in the grave he is avouched to be the prophet and servant of God." And here the chapter's earlier shadows lift. The prophet who died the common death of all men (as v.20 soberly recorded) is vindicated in his very tomb; the God who let him die proves himself the giver of life; and a resurrection worked through dead bones stands as a pledge, to Israel and to every later reader, of the God who raises the dead.
22:
"But Hazael king of Syria oppressed Israel all the days of Jehoahaz." With the miracle told, the historian resumes the political thread, and the witnesses, few and brief here, attend chiefly to the grammar that governs the sentence. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott corrects the tense: the verse is not a fresh statement but a resumption, and should read "Now Hazael had oppressed Israel," a pluperfect that carries the reader back to v.3, where the oppression was first announced. Keil and Delitzsch read it the same way — "Hazael had oppressed" — pointing to the same facts already given at v.7, and treating this notice as the frame within which the coming mercy is set.

The reformed John Gill adds a small chronological gloss, taking "all the days of Jehoahaz" to mean at least the years Hazael reigned and oppressed alone, before Joash was taken to reign with his father in the co-regency he has argued for elsewhere. The point on which the witnesses converge is the function of the verse in the narrative. Having lifted the reader up to the wonder at Elisha's tomb, the writer sets him down again in the long, grinding reality of the reign just closed: a whole generation lived under the heel of Damascus, the oppression that opened with the kindled anger of God and ran unbroken through the seventeen years of Jehoahaz.

That resumption is not idle repetition. By recalling the full weight of the affliction — the disarmament of v.7, the anger of v.3, the whole span of Hazael's dominance — the verse throws into relief the astonishing turn that the next verse will announce. The darker and more unrelenting the oppression, the brighter the grace that God is about to show for the sake of his covenant. The witnesses leave the verse as the historian meant it: a deliberate recollection of how bad things had been, set as the dark ground against which the LORD's compassion, and the recovery of Israel's cities, will presently shine. It is the pause before the reversal, the reminder of the depth from which mercy will lift a people who had done nothing to deserve it.
23:
Against the long oppression the chapter now sets its deepest word of grace — "the LORD was gracious unto them... because of his covenant with Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob" — and the witnesses find here the theological heart of the whole reign. The reformed John Gill marks the wonder that this grace came "notwithstanding their apostasy from him, and the idolatry of the calves they were guilty of": mercy shown to a people who had earned only wrath. Keil and Delitzsch ground it precisely where the text does — for the sake of the covenant made with the patriarchs the LORD turned again to Israel, would not destroy them, and did not yet cast them from his presence.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke lingers, as often, over the Hebrew verbs, and finds in them an ascending tenderness. The first, he says, means God "had tender affection for them, as a husband has for his wife, or a father for his own children"; the second, that "his bowels yearned over them," the very compassion of Jeremiah 31:20, "my bowels are troubled for him; I will surely have mercy upon him, saith the LORD"; the third, that "he turned his face towards them," receiving them again into favour. And Clarke names the deeper reason the covenant secured their survival: from Israel the Messiah must come, and through them the light that should enlighten the Gentiles, so that God would not make a full end of them. Yet he weighs the sombre limit of "as yet": they were spared then, but afterward were indeed cast out, and now "sit in darkness and the shadow of death," the veil upon their face — though, he adds in hope, "if they yet turn to the LORD, the veil shall be taken away," reading the verse through 2 Corinthians 3:14.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott hears in the same phrase the voice of the historian himself. The verse, he judges, is a remark of the compiler, betrayed by its style, its appeal to the covenant, and above all by that "as yet," or "until now" — meaning the day when he was writing, after the northern kingdom had finally perished. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws the moral that reconciles the mercy and the coming judgment: "the slowness of God's process against sinners, even when they remain impenitent, must be construed to the advantage of his mercy, not the impeachment of his justice." The witnesses do not divide; they gather around one confession. Israel is spared not for any worth of her own, nor for any repentance, but because God keeps covenant with the fathers — a forbearance that is real mercy and yet not final impunity, since the "as yet" hangs over it as both reprieve and warning.
24:
"So Hazael the king of Syria died, and Benhadad his son reigned in his stead." The death of the great oppressor is the hinge on which Israel's recovery turns, and the witnesses, though only two are at hand, draw out both the name and the providence in it. The reformed John Gill identifies this Benhadad as the third of the name: the first Benhadad belonged to the older line of Damascene kings, and though the kingdom had now passed into another family with Hazael's usurpation, the name, being honourable among the Syrians, was retained by his son.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds the historical and philological detail. Benhadad III is not mentioned in the Assyrian inscriptions, but his reign synchronises with that of Samas-Rimmon in Assyria, who made no expeditions to the west between about 825 and 812 B.C. — a lull that would leave Syria unsupported and open to Israel's counter-attack. The name Ben-hadad, Ellicott stresses, does not imply any blood connection with the dynasty Hazael overthrew; it means "son of Hadad" and is really a divine title, borne as a throne-name, a point he ties to his note on 2 Kings 6:24. And he judges this Benhadad to have been probably a feebler sovereign than his father, remarking wryly that the maxim "brave sons spring from the brave and good" is as often contradicted as confirmed by experience.

Brief as the verse is, the witnesses let its providence show. The arrows-prophecy of the earlier verses had promised Joash victory over Syria; here the way to that victory is quietly opened. The strong hand of Hazael, under which Israel had been "made like dust for trampling," is stilled by death; a weaker son takes the throne just as the great Assyrian power to the north is looking elsewhere; and into that opening Israel will strike. Neither Gill nor Ellicott labours the point, but it lies plainly in the sequence they expound: God had bound himself to spare his covenant people, and now, without a word of miracle, he arranges the deaths and successions and distant distractions of empires so that the promise given at Elisha's deathbed may be fulfilled. The oppressor dies; a feebler man succeeds; and the door to deliverance swings open at exactly the appointed hour.
25:
The chapter ends where the arrows had pointed — "Three times did Joash beat him, and recovered the cities of Israel" — and the witnesses read the close as the exact, measured fulfilment of Elisha's dying word. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke draws the line straight back to the sign: the three victories were "according to the prediction of Elisha" in v.19, and that they were decisive appears from their fruit, for Joash retook from the Syrians the cities Hazael had seized. Clarke names the wider lesson: "God accomplished his word of judgment, and his word of mercy. The Syrians found themselves to be but men, and the Israelites found they could do nothing without God" — and he adds that in his dealings of justice and mercy alike God aims not only at the deliverance of his own but at the conviction and even the salvation of his enemies.

The witnesses divide, mildly, over which cities were recovered. The reformed John Gill, with Clarke, locates them in Gilead and Bashan, the trans-Jordan territory of Reuben, Gad, and the half-tribe of Manasseh. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott objects on historical grounds: the cities here must have been west of the Jordan, since the trans-Jordan had already been overrun by Hazael back in the days of Jehu, as 2 Kings 10:32 records; the full restoration of the ancient borders, including the eastern lands, would wait for Jeroboam II in 2 Kings 14:25. So the debate is whether Joash's three victories won back the eastern tribes' land or only the western cities, with the greater recovery reserved for his son. Ellicott also fixes the verb, "beat him," as the "smite" of the prophecy in v.19, tying the fulfilment tightly to the sign.

On the theology of the ending the witnesses are wholly one, and the chapter's threads gather here. The victory is real, but it is bounded — three times and no more — precisely as the half-hearted smiting of v.18 had determined; the king who spared his strokes reaps a limited triumph. Yet a limited triumph it truly is: the covenant mercy declared in v.23 takes visible shape in recovered cities, and the God who kindled his anger against Israel proves himself also the God who keeps his word to Abraham. Clarke's double summary stands over the whole: God accomplished both his word of judgment and his word of mercy, and Israel learned, in the very hour of her recovery, that without the LORD she could do nothing at all.
